question: What are the main sections covered under the 'Requirements For All PlayReady Products' in the document PR_Compliance_Rules_Part01_v2021.pdf?
user_input: What are the main sections covered under the 'Requirements For All PlayReady Products' in the document PR_Compliance_Rules_Part01_v2021.pdf?
answer:
The main sections covered under the "Requirements For All PlayReady Products" in the document PR_Compliance_Rules_Part01_v2021.pdf include:

- Scope of the requirements applicable to all PlayReady Products.
- Enforcement of license rights and restrictions based on the type of license (e.g., PlayReady License, Windows Media DRM 10 for Portable Devices, Windows Media DRM 10 for Network Devices).
- Policy enforcement rules depending on flags such as Must Understand and Best Effort.
- Privacy compliance requirements for PlayReady Products.

These sections set forth the foundational compliance rules that apply universally to all PlayReady Products, before more specific functionality requirements covered in later sections of the document【4:0†source】【4:5†source】【4:1†source】【4:4†source】【4:3†source】【4:7†source】【4:9†source】.
response:
The main sections covered under the "Requirements For All PlayReady Products" in the document PR_Compliance_Rules_Part01_v2021.pdf include:

- Scope of the requirements applicable to all PlayReady Products.
- Enforcement of license rights and restrictions based on the type of license (e.g., PlayReady License, Windows Media DRM 10 for Portable Devices, Windows Media DRM 10 for Network Devices).
- Policy enforcement rules depending on flags such as Must Understand and Best Effort.
- Privacy compliance requirements for PlayReady Products.

These sections set forth the foundational compliance rules that apply universally to all PlayReady Products, before more specific functionality requirements covered in later sections of the document【4:0†source】【4:5†source】【4:1†source】【4:4†source】【4:3†source】【4:7†source】【4:9†source】.

question: Under what conditions can a PlayReady Product pass decrypted A/V Content through local outputs?
user_input: Under what conditions can a PlayReady Product pass decrypted A/V Content through local outputs?
answer:
- The PlayReady License, PlayReady-ND Output Protection Level, and Output Control requirements must be met.
- The decrypted A/V Content may only be passed to local Outputs described in the Restricted Outputs and Other Outputs sections, complying with their specific restrictions and controls【4:1†source】【4:0†source】.
response:
- The PlayReady License, PlayReady-ND Output Protection Level, and Output Control requirements must be met.

question: What triggers the Stop State for A/V Content in PlayReady, and what must a PlayReady Product do after entering the Stop State?
user_input: What triggers the Stop State for A/V Content in PlayReady, and what must a PlayReady Product do after entering the Stop State?
answer:
- Stop State for A/V Content occurs when decrypted A/V Content is no longer being Passed, such as when the end user selects a stop playback function, the final portion of decrypted A/V Content has been Passed, or decrypted A/V Content stops being Passed in preparation for different content.  
- After entering Stop State, a PlayReady Product must re-initialize PlayReady before Passing any decrypted A/V Content.  
- Additionally, a PlayReady Product must close and destroy all PlayReady decryptor objects when Stop State occurs【4:2†source】【4:1†source】【4:4†source】【4:0†source】.
response:
- Stop State for A/V Content occurs when decrypted A/V Content is no longer being Passed, such as when the end user selects a stop playback function, the final portion of decrypted A/V Content has been Passed, or decrypted A/V Content stops being Passed in preparation for different content.  
- After entering Stop State, a PlayReady Product must re-initialize PlayReady before Passing any decrypted A/V Content.  
- Additionally, a PlayReady Product must close and destroy all PlayReady decryptor objects when Stop State occurs【4:2†source】【4:1†source】【4:4†source】【4:0†source】.

3.2 
Stop State 
For A/V Content, Stop State occurs when decrypted A/V Content is no longer being Passed, for example 
but without limitation as a result of one of the following: (i) the end user has selected a stop playback 
function, (ii) the final portion of decrypted A/V Content has been Passed, or (iii) decrypted A/V Content 
stops being Passed in preparation for Passing different decrypted A/V Content. After the PlayReady 
Product enters the Stop State for any reason, it must re-initialize PlayReady before Passing any 
decrypted A/V Content. 
3.3 
Secure Stop  
If the Secure Stop Object, whether with or without the Secure Stop 2 Object, is specified in a PlayReady 
License, a PlayReady Product must comply with all requirements in this Section 3.3 (Secure Stop)

A PlayReady Product must implement Secure Stop in a manner Consistent with the

question: What syntax must a PlayReady Product follow when creating PlayReady Licenses?
user_input: What syntax must a PlayReady Product follow when creating PlayReady Licenses?
answer: A PlayReady Product must create only PlayReady Licenses that follow the syntax defined in the "PlayReady DRM Extensible Media Rights Specification."
response: A PlayReady Product must create only PlayReady Licenses that follow the syntax defined in the "PlayReady DRM Extensible Media Rights Specification."
contexts:

6 Creating PlayReady Licenses  
A PlayReady Product may create PlayReady Licenses only if all requirements in this Section 6 (Creating 
PlayReady Licenses) are met. 
6.1 
Extensible Media Rights Specification  
A PlayReady Product must create only PlayReady Licenses that follow the syntax defined in the 
“PlayReady DRM Extensible Media Rights Specification”. 
6.2 
Embedded License Setting  
The Embedded License Settings Object must be specified in the PlayReady License. The allowed values 
for the License Processing Indicator field must be set to values listed in Table 6.2 (Allowed License 
Processing Indicator Values) according to the License type.

question: What are the allowed values for the License Type in PlayReady licenses and what do they signify?
user_input: What are the allowed values for the License Type in PlayReady licenses and what do they signify?
answer:
The allowed values for the License Type in PlayReady licenses and their significance are:

- Root License: License will not be embedded.
- Device Bound License: License will not be embedded.
- Leaf License: License will be embedded.
- Domain Bound License: License will be embedded.

question: What must a PlayReady Product do when creating WMDRM-ND Licenses as a Network Device Transmitter regarding the Cannot Persist bit?
user_input: What must a PlayReady Product do when creating WMDRM-ND Licenses as a Network Device Transmitter regarding the Cannot Persist bit?
answer: A PlayReady Product functioning as a Network Device Transmitter must set the Cannot Persist bit in the Rights field of the Rights Settings Object to a value of 1 when creating WMDRM-ND Licenses.
response: A PlayReady Product functioning as a Network Device Transmitter must set the Cannot Persist bit in the Rights field of the Rights Settings Object to a value of 1 when creating WMDRM-ND Licenses.
contexts:
Compliance Rules for PlayReady Products 
 
 
© 2021 Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved. 
41 
1 November 2021 
8 Creating WMDRM-ND Licenses  
A PlayReady Product may create WMDRM-ND Licenses only if all requirements in this Section 8 
(Creating WMDRM-ND Licenses) are met. 
8.1 
Extensible Media Rights Specification  
A PlayReady Product must create only WMDRM-ND objects that follow the syntax defined in the 
“Windows Media DRM Extensible Media Rights Specification”. 
8.2 
Rights Settings  
If the PlayReady Product is functioning as a Network Device Transmitter, it must set the Cannot Persist 
bit in the Rights field of the Rights Settings Object to a value of 1. 
8.3 
Copy Policy Container 
8.3.1 
The Copy Policy 2 Container Object must not be specified if the Copy Policy Container 
Object is specified in the WMDRM-ND License.  
8.3.2 
The Copy Policy 2 Container Object may be specified in a WMDRM-ND License only if 
the PlayReady Product is a Network Device Transmitter as a Mic

question: What are the allowed minimum copy protection level values specified in the WMDRM-ND License for the Copy Protection Level Restriction Object?
user_input: What are the allowed minimum copy protection level values specified in the WMDRM-ND License for the Copy Protection Level Restriction Object?
answer:
The allowed minimum copy protection level values specified in the WMDRM-ND License for the Copy Protection Level Restriction Object are:

- 300: Copy to PD-DRM devices, WMDRM-PD devices, and PlayReady Portable Devices
- 400: Copy to WMDRM-PD devices and PlayReady Portable Devices
response:

- 400: Copy to WMDRM-PD devices and PlayReady Portable Devices

question: What key usage must a PlayReady-ND Receiver's Device Certificate contain for a PlayReady Product to transmit to it?
user_input: What key usage must a PlayReady-ND Receiver's Device Certificate contain for a PlayReady Product to transmit to it?
answer: A PlayReady-ND Receiver's Device Certificate must contain the key usage rights EncryptKey, PRNDEncrypt, and Sign, with the keys for these usages being individually unique for a PlayReady Product to transmit to it.
response: A PlayReady-ND Receiver's Device Certificate must contain the key usage rights EncryptKey, PRNDEncrypt, and Sign, with the keys for these usages being individually unique for a PlayReady Product to transmit to it.

Key Usage 
A PlayReady Product must not Transmit to a PlayReady-ND Receiver whose Device Certificate 
does not contain the PlayReady-ND Encrypt key usage or whose Device Certificate does not 
contain the Receiver feature. 
11.2 
Timer  
The PlayReady Product must implement a timer with a resolution of no coarser than one (1) 
millisecond.

Security Level Verification 
A PlayReady Product must verify that the Certificate Security Level in the Device Certificate of

question: What are the specific requirements for proximity detection in PlayReady regarding RTT, TTL, and Hop Count?
user_input: What are the specific requirements for proximity detection in PlayReady regarding RTT, TTL, and Hop Count?
answer:
- The Round Trip Time (RTT) between the PlayReady Product and the associated PlayReady-ND Receiver must be no more than seven (7) milliseconds as measured with a timer.  
- The Time To Live (TTL) must be set to three (3) in the IPv4 header of the proximity challenge message when transmitting over IPv4 networks.  
- The Hop Count must be set to three (3) in the IPv6 header of the proximity challenge message when transmitting over IPv6 networks.
response:
- The Round Trip Time (RTT) between the PlayReady Product and the associated PlayReady-ND Receiver must be no more than seven (7) milliseconds as measured with a timer.  
- The Time To Live (TTL) must be set to three (3) in the IPv4 header of the proximity challenge message when transmitting over IPv4 networks.  
- The Hop Count must be set to three (3) in the IPv6 header of the proximity challenge message when transmitting over IPv6 networks.
contexts:
yReady-ND Receiver is not less than the minimum License Security Level of the 
PlayReady License for the requested PlayReady A/V Content. If this verification fails, the 
PlayReady Product must not Transmit to the associated PlayReady-ND Receiver. 
11.4 
Proximity Detection Policy 
11.4.1 Round Trip Time (RTT) Verification  
A PlayReady Product must verify that the RTT between the PlayReady Product and the 
associated PlayReady-ND Receiver, as measured, is no more than seven (7) 
milliseconds computed with a timer as described in Section 11.2 (Timer). 
11.4.2 Time To Live (TTL) 
A PlayReady Product must set the TTL to three (3) in the IPv4 header of the proximity 
challenge message when Transmitting over IPv4 networks. 
11.4.3 Hop Count 
A PlayReady Product must set the Hop Count to three (3) in the IPv6 header of the 
proximity challenge message when Transmitting over IPv6 networks.

A PlayReady Product may Transmit to a PlayReady-ND Rece

ground_truth: The PlayReady Product must verify that the Round Trip Time (RTT) is no more than seven (7) milliseconds. It must set the Time To Live (TTL) to three (3) in the IPv4 header and set the Hop Count to three (3) in the IPv6 header of the proximity challenge message.
